hero.lead: Puntovivo brings sales, inventory and purchasing into a single desktop console. Fast scanning, blind close, inter-site transfers and split payment — all under one design language. Open source, in active development, with a demo available.

features.title: Six modules, one design language.
features.desc: Every surface shares the same grid, the same editorial serif and the same calm blue. The register feels like a digital notebook, not a control panel.

items.title: Friction-free checkout
items.desc: Scan, search by SKU or barcode and adjust quantities without opening modals. Checkout splits cash, card, transfer, credit and other methods on a single screen.

items.title: Register with blind close
items.desc: Open with a count by denomination, log cash-in and cash-out movements during the shift, and close blind — the expected balance stays hidden until you submit the final count.

items.title: Inventory per site
items.desc: Per-site balances as the source of truth. Movements, physical counts, opening inventory and stock lookups stay in lockstep with the tenant aggregate.

items.title: Inter-site transfers
items.desc: Send now and receive later with an in-transit state. Receiving discrepancies are logged with a note, and a cancellation reverses balances instantly.

items.title: Purchasing, orders & quotes
items.desc: Receive partially or fully against an order, return stock to the supplier, and issue quotes from the same catalog. Every movement updates the product's cost history.

items.title: Multi-tenant & audit
items.desc: Per-tenant isolation, per-site sequentials and a per-shift audit log. Role or tenant changes invalidate the session without interrupting a sale in progress.

caja.titleB: reconciles itself — no guessing for the cashier.
caja.desc: Open the shift counting bill by bill, log every drawer cash-in or cash-out through the day, and close blind: the expected balance stays hidden until you submit the final count. Any difference lands in the recent-closes report — not a separate notebook.
caja.openDesc: Base counted by denomination · 3×$50,000, 3×$10,000. Matches the register template.
caja.skimDesc: Drawer surplus sent to the safe. Note: "Skim 11:30 — partial cut".
caja.blindDesc: The expected balance stays hidden until you submit the final count by denomination. The difference is computed on save.

ai.titleB: — not a chatbot bolted on the side.
ai.desc: Ask in plain language, let it watch your cashiers, search products by meaning, dictate a supplier invoice with the camera. All inside the same console, with the same per-shift audit. All four capabilities ship turned off: enable them from Settings › AI whenever you want.
ai.coreMeta: co-pilot · anomalies · semantic search · OCR · 4 optional capabilities
ai.copilotTitleEm: It answers with data, not text.
ai.copilotLead: Type what you want to know in plain language — "best-selling products", "top cashier", "sites with low margin". Puntovivo builds the SQL, runs it on your tenant, and returns the answer, the table and the chart. The SQL stays visible so any manager can audit it.

topProducts.q: What are the 10 best-selling products of the last month by quantity and amount?
topProducts.ans: At Sede Norte the top sellers last month are Ground beef 500g · 37 Toilet paper x12 · 33 Doria Spaghetti · 32 and seven more. Combined they total $5.73M across 282 units.

cashierTicket.q: Which cashier has the best average ticket this week across all sites?
cashierTicket.ans: Carolina Cajera (Sede Norte) leads with an average ticket of $48,200 · 84 tickets +23% vs team followed by María R. (Centro) at $39,140. Andrés (Centro) closes the pack at $24,100.

marginDairy.q: Which sites have low margin on dairy over the last 7 days?
marginDairy.ans: Sede Sur has a margin of 14% · dairy −8 pts vs average over the last 7 days. The most affected SKU is Cream 250ml, with cost raised last week. Norte and Centro stay healthy.

cards.anomaliesDesc: We cross each cashier against their own 30-day average and against the rest of the team. When something breaks the pattern — a ticket spike, an unusual return, suspicious discounts — it reaches your inbox with a severity and the raw context.
cards.anomaliesFoot: A starting point, not an accusation · mute 7 days · per-shift audit
cards.semanticDesc: The catalog is indexed with embeddings on every sync. Search "low-fat dairy" and Yogurt Alpina light shows up even though the SKU says "light" nowhere. Handy for cashiers who know a product by its description, not its exact name.
cards.semanticFoot: Local embeddings · regenerated when you edit prices/descriptions
cards.ocrDesc: Upload a photo of the supplier invoice — crooked, smudged, handwritten where allowed. Puntovivo extracts supplier, lines, quantities, VAT and total, and pre-fills the purchase screen so you only confirm.
cards.ocrFoot: Upload file · take photo · 5 fields extracted · matched to SKUs

roadmap.title: More capabilities on the way.
roadmap.desc: AI in Puntovivo isn't a separate module — it's a cross-cutting layer. Every release touches at least one of these verbs: predict, summarize, search or extract.

aiFaq.title: What owners and managers ask before turning the AI on.
aiFaq.desc: Privacy, cost, what happens offline and how to turn it off. If these six answers don't convince you, leave it off — the register works just the same without it.

items.q: Can the AI see my customers' sensitive data?
items.a: No. Before any question reaches the model, Puntovivo redacts names, ID numbers, emails, phones and card numbers. The SQL runs on your local database with a read-only user — the model sees the question and the tabulated result, never the raw database or the PII. The redaction policy is recorded in the per-shift audit log.

items.q: Where does the model run? Does my data leave the country?
items.a: The Co-pilot calls whichever AI provider you configure per tenant (OpenAI, Anthropic or Ollama) — the model runs in that provider's cloud, not inside Puntovivo. Everything else stays local: the SQL is built and executed against your own database, and the catalog embeddings are computed and stored at the site, so semantic search never leaves your network. Anomalies are 100% classic statistics: no language model required.

items.q: What happens if the site loses internet?
items.a: The register keeps charging — the AI never blocks sales. The Co-pilot and invoice OCR rely on the cloud provider, so offline they pause with an amber banner; semantic search uses the last local embedding index, so it keeps finding products by meaning even offline. Anomalies are computed in batch the next time you sync.

items.q: How much does the AI cost?
items.a: Puntovivo doesn't charge you for the AI: you use your own account with the provider you configure (OpenAI, Anthropic or Ollama) and pay that provider directly for usage. With Ollama you can run a local model and pay nothing per token. To avoid surprises, every tenant has configurable usage quotas that cap spend — but that's a limit you set, not a bill from us. Semantic search and anomalies don't consume tokens.

items.q: Can I trust the answers? What if it makes up numbers?
items.a: That's why we always show you the executed SQL and the row count below. The narrative answer is a summary of the tabulated result — not of the model's knowledge. If you want to be strict: the manager role can enable "hard audit" mode, which hides the narrative answer and shows you only the table. The AI proposes; you decide.

items.q: How do I turn the AI on or off?
items.a: All four capabilities ship turned off. From Settings › AI you enable them with a switch per capability: Co-pilot, anomalies, semantic search and OCR turn on or off individually and per site. A tenant that leaves them off gets the exact same console — no chips, no sparkles, no chats.

alerts.title: What's happening across all your sites, in one view.
alerts.desc: Low stock, closes with a difference, transfers in transit, refunds. Puntovivo cross-references data per site and only interrupts you when it's worth it.
alerts.narrativeTitle: Don't check three consoles. Check one.
alerts.narrativeDesc: The dashboard gathers sales, inventory, shifts and syncs into one feed. Every alert takes you to the exact spot in the console — one click, not three tabs.

items.d: Coffee 500g, long-life milk, white corn flour, 80mm thermal paper — all below the configured minimum.
Camilo's shift closed short against the expected balance. The difference is logged in recent closes.
Sede Centro sent 28 units of coffee 500g. Pending receipt at Sede Sur.
Equimercado delivered 28 of 40 units. The remaining 12 stay pending on the order.
$1.84M across 47 tickets · average ticket $39,140 · 30-day revenue window.
Lina refunded the sale. Stock restored across the 3 lines and excluded from completed revenue.

flow.title: The cashier's loop, in four steps.
flow.desc: At most five actions per screen. The console doesn't teach — the cashier already knows how to charge. Puntovivo just gets out of the way.

steps.p: Count the opening base by denomination. The register is ready to charge right away.
SKU, barcode, quantities and discounts without opening unnecessary modals.
Cash, card, transfer, credit — or a split payment that adds up to the total. The receipt prints at 58 or 80 mm.
Count the drawer by denomination. The system hides the expected balance until you submit the final count.

modules.title: What's already built into the console.
modules.desc: The POS, cash, inventory and purchasing core is real code you can run today. DIAN electronic invoicing and physical hardware are still on the roadmap.

bullets: Co-pilot: plain-language questions about your data · auditable SQL · chart + table.
Anomalies detected per cashier against their own 30-day average.
Semantic catalog search with local embeddings — find by meaning, not exact SKU.
Invoice OCR: photo → supplier, lines, VAT and total pre-filled into purchasing.
Opening with a count by denomination against the register template.
Shift movements: cash-in, cash-out, drop to safe, change replenishment.
Blind close — expected balance hidden until you submit the final count.
Cash-control report with active sessions and differences to review.
In-transit mode: the origin site is debited now, the destination on receipt.
Partial receipt with discrepancy notes and a history badge.
Cancellation with immediate reversal of both balances.
Combine cash, card, transfer, credit and other methods.
Optional reference per payment for reconciliation.
Exact-sum validation before confirming the charge.
Staged receipt with per-line progress against the order.
Purchase returns with restock and an auditable reason.
Quotes from the same catalog and selling unit.

pricing.title: Open source. Self-host it yourself, for free.
pricing.desc: Puntovivo is MIT-licensed open-source software. The self-hosted version is free forever: download the desktop app or build it from source. A managed cloud is on the way, with no pricing yet.

self.name: Self-hosted
self.desc: Run Puntovivo on your own computer or server. POS, inventory, purchasing, multi-site and multi-tenant — all included, no license to pay.
bullets: Registers and users with no license cap
POS, inventory, purchasing and transfers
Blind close + cash control
Multi-tenant with per-tenant isolation
Optional AI with your own provider

cloud.name: Managed cloud
cloud.desc: A hosted version for anyone who doesn't want to run their own server: managed backups, updates and syncing. It isn't ready yet and has no defined price.
bullets: Managed hosting and backups
Automatic updates
Inter-site syncing
No server of your own to run

faq.title: What owners and cashiers ask before switching systems.

items.q: What does a site need to get started?
items.a: A computer running Windows 10+, macOS 12+ or a recent Linux. The desktop app runs right there and the cloud isn't required to sell. Thermal printing, a cash drawer and USB scanners are on the roadmap — we haven't validated physical hardware yet, so today the demo is on screen.
Yes. Changes made offline stay local and sync when the network returns. The app's status bar shows pending changes and conflicts to resolve — none of them blocks a sale in progress.

items.q: How do I handle several sites or registers?
items.a: Puntovivo is multi-site and multi-tenant from day one. Each site has its own sale sequence, its own inventory and its own independent register. The selector in the top bar switches the active site without signing out.

items.q: What roles does the system have?
items.a: Admin, manager, cashier (sales) and viewer. Every console route — dashboard, sales, purchasing, settings — explicitly declares which roles can see it, and changing roles invalidates the session instantly.

items.q: How does the blind close work?
items.a: When closing the shift, the cashier counts the drawer by denomination. The system keeps the expected balance hidden until the counted total is submitted. The difference (balanced, over or short) is logged in the cash-control report — nobody fixes it by hand.

Yes, via a CSV / Excel import with SKU, description, cost, price, VAT and stock per warehouse — that's the path that already works. Automatic per-system mappers (Loyverse, Alegra, Siigo, World Office) are on the roadmap; for now you export to CSV and import that.

cta.title: A register that respects your store's rhythm.
cta.desc: Try it today: download the desktop app or build it from source. It's open source, in active development, and there's a demo ready for you to see it running.

footer.tagline: POS, inventory and purchasing for LatAm retail. Open source, local-first, bilingual.

sobre.titleA: An open-source POS built in Colombia,
sobre.lead: Puntovivo is a personal project by Johnny IV Young Ospino: the point-of-sale system I wish I'd had for Colombian and wider LatAm retail. Open source, MIT-licensed, in active development.
sobre.heroNote: No venture capital, no team, no invented customers. An open-source project moving forward commit by commit.
sobre.manifestoP1: I made Puntovivo because I watched too many cashiers fight their system on a Friday at 8 p.m. Cluttered screens, modal windows, stock counters that lie. The register is the heart of the business; it deserves respect, not more friction.
sobre.manifestoP2: The commitment is simple: five actions per screen, max, real neutral Spanish, and a Friday close that isn't an event. Everything else — optional AI, multi-site, transfers — is built on top of that, never on top of the cashier.
sobre.valuesTitle: The six things I never let go of.

values.d: If a module needs a tutorial, the module is wrong. I remove buttons before I add captions.
The sale closes without internet. Syncing is an implementation detail, not a condition.
It's not translation: every line of copy is written for a neighborhood cashier, with no voseo and no forced regionalisms. If it sounds off, it's out.
Every shift, role, site or price change is logged. Trust is earned by showing the ledger.
All the code is open-source MIT. Anyone who wants to review how VAT or the close is calculated can do it on GitHub.
Solid POS core, but not production-certified. DIAN invoicing is a mock and hardware isn't validated — I say it plainly, I don't hide it.

sobre.timelineTitle: What it already does, and what it doesn't yet.

timeline.d: Register with SKU search and split tender, blind close by denomination, per-site inventory, transfers, purchasing, quotes and returns. Multi-tenant with a per-shift audit log.
Co-pilot natural language → read-only SQL, anomaly detection per cashier, semantic search and invoice OCR. All off by default; you enable it in Settings › AI.
Today DIAN issuance is only a mock. Real issuance needs a contract with an authorized Technology Provider plus certificates — it's on the roadmap, not ready.
ESC/POS thermal printer, cash drawer and USB scanner aren't validated. Payment terminals are manual for now.
A hosted version with managed backups and updates. It doesn't exist yet; today Puntovivo is self-hosted.

sobre.teamDesc: Puntovivo is developed by me alone. There's no team, no offices, no investors. If you want to contribute, the code is on GitHub and issues and pull requests are welcome.

team.b: The developer behind Puntovivo. Design, engineering, documentation and this site — the whole project is the work of a single person, in the open.

sobre.ctaTitle: Want to contribute or try the project?
sobre.ctaDesc: Open an issue on GitHub, post in Discussions or send me an email. Every contribution is welcome.

docs.lead: A single source for cashiers, managers and developers. The docs grow alongside the project, in the open.

categories.d: Install the desktop app, register your first site and ring up your first sale in under 20 minutes.
Charge, discount, refund, split payments, suspend and resume a sale, and generate the receipt.
Opening by denominations, shift movements, blind close and the cash-control report.
Products, variants, cost history, physical counts, adjustments, minimums and shrinkage.
Send, receive partially, void. How per-site balance looks while it's in transit.

popular.t: Set up a 58 mm thermal printer
Run a blind close with a difference

docs.exampleKicker: This is what a page looks like
docs.exampleTitle: An article, with no extra decoration.
docs.exampleDesc: Permanent sidebar on the left, on-this-page on the right, content given room to breathe in the middle. Zero modal windows, zero cookie pop-ups in the docs.
docs.artTitle: What is a blind close and why does it matter?
docs.artLead: A blind close is the way the console asks you to count the drawer without showing you the expected balance. That keeps you from “tidying up” the count and leaves the difference visible to the owner.
docs.calloutText: The cashier counts bill by bill, submits, and only then sees whether it came out balanced, over or short.
docs.artFlowP: When it's time to close the register, the console shows a screen with slots to count each denomination. The expected balance — what the system computed from sales, discounts, refunds and shift movements — stays hidden.
docs.artH2Diff: What happens if there's a difference
docs.artDiffP1: The difference is recorded in the cash-control report, not in a separate notebook. The system marks it
docs.artDiffP5: and stores the who-and-when in the log.
docs.tableSquaredMeaning: The count matches the expected balance.
docs.tableOverAction: Check for sales that weren't recorded.
docs.tableUnderAction: Check for withdrawals without a note or recent refunds.

roadmap.lead: Each item is prioritized by the real pain of the retail I know. If an idea isn't here yet, I'm still researching it — and you can propose it on GitHub.

now.label: Now
now.desc: In active development · arriving in the next 6 weeks.

items.d: Suggest a transfer between sites, a supplier order or a price adjustment — with one click to execute.
Reconcile the shift balance against the site's safe balance. Difference visible to the manager.
Load SKU, VAT, cost and stock with a single XLSX. Detects duplicates by barcode.
Generates a short link to the receipt and sends it to the customer with one tap. Keeps the thermal one for whoever prefers it.

next.label: Next
next.desc: Next quarter · scope confirmed, design in progress.

items.d: Credit sale with installments, optional WhatsApp reminders and a per-site receivables report.
Direct support for USB/serial scales. Products sold by weight go into the cart without a modal.
Read-only version to review sales and alerts on a phone. Close and register stay on desktop.
Cross-checks electronic payments against the day's sales. Flags differences for review.

later.label: Later
later.desc: Under research · we want to do it but don't yet know how.

items.d: Issue and look up documents without going through a middleman. Requires certification; exploring a signing provider.
Restaurant mode with a floor plan, kitchen tickets, tips and bill splitting.
Let partners publish integrations (accountants, e-commerce, loyalty).
Touchscreen for customers to build their own order. Useful in high-traffic bakeries and ice-cream shops.

roadmap.shippedTitle: What already works in the console.
roadmap.voteDesc: The roadmap is ordered by real retail pain, not hype. If something fights you every Friday, tell me: open an issue on GitHub and it enters the project backlog.

contacto.lead: Puntovivo is open source and built by a single person. There's no sales team and no 24/7 support: the real channels are GitHub and a direct email.
contacto.asideFoot: As a solo project, response times vary. The fastest path is to open an issue on GitHub.
contacto.channelsDesc: These are the project's real channels. Write directly: the person who answers is the one who writes the code.

Open an issue describing the bug or proposal with its context.

atajos.lead: Puntovivo is operated keyboard-first. This page is the cheat sheet: filter by module, hover over a shortcut and watch it light up on the keyboard.
atajos.keyboardFoot: Hover over any shortcut to light up the matching keys.
atajos.emptyText: No shortcut matches that search. Try another word or change the module.

migracion.lead: The path that works today is importing your catalog from a CSV or Excel file. Per-system automatic mappers (Loyverse, Alegra, Siigo, World Office) are on the roadmap; for now you export to CSV from your system and import that into Puntovivo.

sources.d: Popular POS in coffee shops and kiosks.
Accounting + POS for small businesses in Colombia.
Accounting suite with an inventory module.
If it exports to CSV, you import it the same way.

migracion.pickerFootEm: · via CSV · estimated time: 30 to 90 min
migracion.stepsTitle: What migrating takes, step by step.

steps.d: From your current system, export the catalog to CSV or XLSX. It must include SKU or barcode, description, cost, price, VAT and stock per site.

checks.t: Prices excluding VAT or with VAT marked
Stock per site in separate columns

steps.d: Puntovivo deduplicates by barcode and then by SKU. If you sell by weight or by unit, leave the matching column marked. Delete discontinued products before importing.

checks.t: Sale unit: unit, kg, pound or pack

steps.t: Upload the CSV to the importer
steps.d: From the console, open Settings → Import. Drop the CSV with the expected headers and confirm the column mapping field by field before continuing.
Before applying, you'll see the summary: how many new products, how many will be updated, how many need your decision. Approve in batches — nothing is applied until you confirm.
Do a physical count of the main site and compare it against the imported balance. The first register opening uses the base you define, not the imported balance. From there on, the flow is normal.

checks.t: Physical count done at the main site
Opening with a base you define

migracion.compatDesc: This is what automatic mapping will look like when it lands: the wizard would recognize these columns without you renaming anything. For now you export to CSV and match the headers to the format below.
migracion.sampleTitle: Coming from Excel? This is the format.
migracion.sampleDesc: One sheet per site, headers in the first row, UTF-8. Copy this example and replace the data.
migracion.sampleFootA: VAT zero? Leave the column at

faq.q: How long does it take to migrate a single site?
faq.a: If your file comes in clean, between 30 and 90 minutes from export to the first test sale. Adding more sites adds 10-15 minutes per site.

faq.q: Do I have to stop selling while I migrate?
faq.a: No. We recommend migrating after hours and leaving the first register opening for the next day, but the old system can stay active. Just make it clear to the team which one is the real one.

faq.q: And if my catalog has 20,000 SKUs?
faq.a: The importer processes up to 50,000 lines per file. Very large catalogs we split by category and apply in batches — the wizard proposes it on its own.

faq.q: What happens to my sales history?
faq.a: Sales history isn't migrated: you start clean in Puntovivo. You keep your reports from the old system as an archive. For most small businesses, this is what accountants recommend.

migracion.ctaDesc: If your export has an odd structure, open an issue on GitHub with a sample (no sensitive data) and we'll work through it in the open.
